text: The surgeon begins the procedure by making a medial peritoneal opening above the promontory, exposing the area for mesh fixation. The left ureter and superior rectal artery are identified in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the procedure by making a medial peritoneal opening above the promontory, exposing the area for mesh fixation. The left ureter and superior rectal artery are identified and visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects the left ureter, maintaining proximity to the rectum to avoid injury while dividing the left peritoneal reflection. Anterior dissection is challenging due to a fibrotic scar extending from the pouch of Douglas to the rectovaginal septum, resulting from previous perineal procedures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the ureter, staying close to the rectum to avoid injury while dividing the left side of the peritoneal reflection. Anterior dissection is challenging due to a fibrotic scar extending from the pouch of Douglas to the rectovaginal septum, resulting from previous perineal procedures.

The second assistant applies counter-traction from inside the vagina, aiding in the identification of the correct dissection plane and minimizing the risk of injury to the rectal wall and vaginal vault during the laparoscopic mesh rectopexy.

The second assistant applies additional counter-traction from inside the vagina, aiding in the identification of the correct dissection plane and minimizing the risk of injury to the rectal wall and vaginal vault.

The surgeon completes full rectal mobilization through posterior and lateral dissection, preserving lateral stocks. The second assistant applies cephalic traction to assess the complete reduction of the prolapsed rectum.

The surgeon completes full rectal mobilization through posterior and lateral dissection, while preserving lateral stalks. The second assistant applies cephalic traction to assess the complete reduction of the prolapsed rectum.

The surgeon performs sacral rectopexy using a polypropylene mesh strip, securing it with two O-proline sutures to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum and the sacrum. The mesh is carefully positioned to avoid full thickness bites in the rectum and prevent injury to the ureters and iliac vessels during suturing.

The surgeon performs sacral rectopexy using a polypropylene mesh strip, securing it with two O-proline sutures to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum and the sacrum. The mesh is carefully sutured to avoid full thickness bites in the rectum and prevent injury to the ureters and iliac vessels.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneal opening using two absorbable V-lock running sutures to prevent contact between the mesh and the small bowel.
